race.name: California Gold Rush
race.tagline: 45 kilometers of freestyle racing at Royal Gorge in the Sierra Nevada — the ASM Series finale where the West Coast's fastest skaters chase gold in the mountains.

vitals.aid_stations: Aid stations with drinks and energy food

climate.description: Late March at 7,000 feet in the Sierra Nevada brings spring skiing conditions. Temperatures range from -5°C to 10°C with strong sun. The Sierra snowpack is typically deep by late March, but warm spring conditions can soften trails by midday. UV exposure is intense.
challenges: Spring snow conditions — hard and fast in the morning, soft by afternoon
Intense high-altitude UV exposure requires sun protection
Sierra storms can still deliver significant snowfall in late March
Altitude around 7,000 feet affects aerobic performance
Wax selection critical as temperatures change rapidly through the race

course.primary: Loop course at Royal Gorge Cross Country Ski Resort, one of the largest XC ski resorts in North America. Rolling terrain through Sierra Nevada forest with panoramic mountain views. The resort's extensive trail system provides varied terrain with punchy climbs and fast descents.
course.surface: Machine-groomed trails at Royal Gorge XC Resort
course.grooming: Resort-quality grooming with skating lanes on purpose-built trails
features: Royal Gorge is one of North America's largest XC ski resorts
Sierra Nevada mountain panoramas throughout the course
Spring skiing conditions with strong sun and fast snow

nordic_lab_rating.score_note: The Royal Gorge resort setting and Sierra Nevada scenery are excellent. High altitude adds genuine physiological challenge. The ASM Series finale gives it strategic importance for series contenders. Modest field size and limited international draw.

history.summary: The California Gold Rush takes its name from the state's famous 1849 gold rush and serves as the season-ending finale of the American Ski Marathon Series. Held at Royal Gorge Cross Country Ski Resort — one of the largest XC ski areas in North America — the race offers a spectacular Sierra Nevada setting. The freestyle-only format attracts skating enthusiasts looking to close out the racing season on fast spring snow.
notable_facts: American Ski Marathon Series season finale
Held at Royal Gorge, one of North America's largest XC ski resorts
Name references California's 1849 Gold Rush history
Spring conditions often provide fast, firm morning snow
Donner Pass area is steeped in Western frontier history

videos.title: 3 People Killed After Jeep Falls Off Cliff
videos.description: Two nurses and their tour guide driver were killed after their Jeep plunged over a cliff along Camp Bird Road in the Colorado mountains. Arizona tourists Diana Robles, Ofelia Figueroa-Perez and their driver, Don Fehd, were all killed when their 2022 Jeep plunged 100 feet off a cliff. The 28-year-old and her aunt were both dedicated nurses from Yuma. Before the crash, Robles took a picture of the iconic Imogene Pass.

videos.title: Bikes On Ice- A 2,000 km winter cycling expedition in the Yukon and Alaska
videos.description: In this funny, engaging and intimate adventure documentary, three men spend 49 days cycling 2000 km on the frozen Yukon River and Bering Sea from Dawson To Nome, retracing the route of two gold miners who did the same journey by bike 100 years earlier. The journey took place in 2003, in the pre-fat bike era.

videos.title: Chapter 05 - Babbitt by Sinclair Lewis
videos.description:
Chapter 5. Classic Literature VideoBook with synchronized text, interactive transcript, and closed captions in multiple languages. Audio courtesy of Librivox. Read by Mike Vendetti.

Playlist for Babbitt by Sinclair Lewis: http://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL752595FA3E1D6D74

Babbitt free audiobook at Librivox: http://librivox.org/babbitt-by-sinclair-lewis/

Babbitt free eBook at Project Gutenberg: http://www.gutenberg.org/ebooks/1156

Babbitt at Wikipedia: http://goo.gl/986Dr

View a list of all our videobooks: http://www.ccprose.com/booklist

videos.title: Frisco holding 54th annual Gold Rush Nordic Races
videos.description: The Town of Frisco is holding its 54th annual Gold Rush Nordic Races today.
videos.transcript:
also happening today the town of Frisco
is holding their 54th annual Gold Rush
Nordic races 54 straight years makes
this the longest continually running
Nordic ski race in the country now they
have something for everyone with a 23k
skate ski race a 10K skate and a 10K
Classic Plus a 5k skate and 1K for the
kids it's also worth just going to watch
it's very welcoming for spectators and
the fact that every race starts and
finishes is in the same place um it has
a a very a festival kind of a party feel
to it bring it on the 23k starts at 10
but day of registration it's actually
open until 11:15 this morning so if
you're interested get yourself up to
slopeside Hall across from the frisco
Nordic Center I will say skate skiing is
a lot harder than it looks I tried it
this year I was falling all over the
place all right we'll be right back

videos.title: California's Gold   S01E12   Lost Sierra
videos.description:
Join Huell and travel to Downieville, nestled high in the Sierra Nevada mountains, to see what makes this little town so special. Explore a town that stood during the years of the California Gold Rush; meet the staff on the Mountain Messenger, the state’s oldest weekly newspaper; and watch a demonstration of the long, heavy wooden skis worn by the gold miners in the mid-1800s.

California Gold Rush Dave Nelson Downieville Lee Adams Liz Fisher long skiis Lost Sierra Mountain Messenger Plumas County Plumas-Eureka State Park Sierra County skiis snow shoe races Virginia Collins
videos.transcript:
This program airs statewide on
California public television and is a
California's gold classic. This series
is endorsed by the California Teachers
Association, the California School
Boards Association, and the California
Library Association.
Hi, I'm Hule Hower trying to walk on a
rather frozen gold lake here in Sierra
County in the northeastern part of
California. Now, this county has a rich
history to it because back in the 1850s
and 1860s, this was all gold rush
country. There were over 12,000 miners
living here. Well, today, Sierra County
is the second smallest county in the
entire state populationwise.
Only 3,000 people live here, and this
place is over 75% national forest.
Because of its rather isolated location
and its history, this whole area is
known as the Lost Sierra. And this is
where we're going to be traveling for
the next couple of days as we go in
search of California's gold.
Here I come right back where I started.
[Applause]
Everything
[Music]
that's
hardly
open up that golden gate. California
here.
Right back where I started from.
They're called the Sierra But 8600 ft
above sea level and spectacular. We got

connect with State Route 49, which would

beginning to think we'd never get there.
And then all of a sudden we looked off
to the side and there it was nestled
down in a quiet valley. Now Downville is
the county seat of Sierra County and
almost 400 people call it home. And
driving into town, we knew immediately
we were in for a treat. The main street
lined with beautiful old buildings
dating back to the 1850s.
Back in the 1850s, Downville was a boom
town caught right in the middle of gold
rush fever. Over 5,000 miners lived
here. Set in one of the most rugged
regions in the state, it was populated
by rugged people. All of them hoping to

Now, we pulled into town not knowing

No. know people like to pollute here. I

Now, you just told me something that I

No, no addresses. We have post office

Well, I I always say it's a sweet little

It's And yet it's a real town. It is the

Now, we went to Downville because we

without trying to sound too corny, after
we had been there a few days, we
discovered the real richness in this
little community is in the people who
call it home. A little bit of
California's gold, nestled in the Lost
Sierra.
Hi Liz.
Hey Joe.
How's it going?
It's great.

pike pole.

No, I don't think so.
Well, see what you think of this one
Money either. When Joe Sharp turns in

It's called the Mountain Messenger, and

This is it.

themselves.
You know, my background is in

That's what I did for 18 years before I

and now it's just me and so I can um

Oh, yeah.
Yeah. We focus on local news. Uh I only

Yeah, we always put wedding stories of

Everybody wants to know and everybody

So, it's top news.

These are the actual papers from a

Goodness gracious.
Uh-huh.

Oh, look. When you do your hundred years

Yes, I do the proof reading, too. So if

and I have to correct them.

Oh, yes, of course they do. Of course

And during the crunch, we just do well

Are you aware constantly that this is

I don't think about it every living

A hundred years ago, the mountain
messenger noted that Jack Barrow was
down from the Phoenix Mine near Sierra
City and reported some fine looking
quartz there. It also printed the stage
coach departures from Downville for
Sierra City in Mary'sville. And right on
the front page was an ad for the Miners
Drugstore.
Today, the same newspaper reports that
Victor and Jean Frink traveled to
Sacramento for the wedding of their son
Dale to Lindy Mason. There's also news
about a quilt show and a Republican
meeting and the goodbye dinner for
Sandra Loving. You can read columns from
Joe Sharp and Judith Miller and poetry
from Virginia. There's a letter to the
editor from Walt Macintosh about the
senseless shooting of a bear and a front
page warning to local youngsters about
dangerous river ice. Now, it's a small
paper, all right, with just two rooms,
two employees, and a spoiled cat. And on
Wednesday Crunch days, things do get
hectic.
But the Mountain Messenger is carrying
on a 150year-old Sierra County tradition
of reporting the news.
It's fast, it's fun, and it's one of our
favorite winter pastimes here in
California. And nowadays, it's
state-of-the-art, too. With snow plows
and ski lifts and all the conveniences,
thousands of happy skiers can be found
on slopes like these in Squall Valley in
the Lake Tahoe basin. Now these days,
it's all kind of glamorous, but it
wasn't always this way.
Are they ready or what? And what we're
looking at is history in the making.
Because back in the mid 1800s, just 70
miles north of the present- day Squall
Valley Ski Resort, the first organized
ski racing in the entire Western
Hemisphere took place when some rugged
and rather adventuresome gold miners
strapped on 14 foot long skis and hit
the slopes. And as part of our lost
Sierra adventure, we traveled from
Sierra County to adjoining Plumis County
to visit the area where all this took
place over 130 years ago. Specifically,
we headed up to Plumis Eureka State Park
near the tiny community of Johnsville.
Our group included Elellanena Red, a
Plumis County resident whose late
husband was a world famous long skier.
We were met by park superintendent Dave
Nelson, who gave us a tour of the ski
museum they've set up and filled us in
on the history of those early California
skiers. Dave, how did this whole thing
get started? Well, back in the 1850s,
gold was discovered in this area, and it
brought a lot of of people into this
area that really weren't used to the
harsh environments that we have on the
eastern side of the Sierra here. Uh, one
of the hardest things to get used to
were the heavy snows that we get during
the winter time. U, so what ended up
developing was the longboards or or
snowshoes as they called them, which
were actually real long skis that they
used just out of practicality to get
around from one mining camp to the
other.
Were they good skiers?
They were excellent skiers. In fact, uh
you know, they uh they started racing
and as because of of the mining camps
being they like to gamble on just about
anything they they could, they started
having races and and of course the
purses ended up being for substantial
amounts of money. And for that reason,
there were people that just ended up
being professional skiers. But it wasn't
just the the men that raced. The women
would race also and and even the
children and the Chinese mine workers
would race and this was really different
than than most activities in the mining
community. Very rarely were women uh
allowed to participate in some of the
other social activities. Now we're
standing in front of this display here
which prominently
uh shows the the the ski dope which was
an intricral part of of the miners's
secret to winning. Right.
It was a very intricral part. Uh in fact
uh people that were known as dope kings
developed and and they literally made a
living just going from mining camp to
mining camp um using their special dopes
that they would not share with other
people. When you had purses of $1500 to
$200 on some of these races, uh that was
four or five months pay for a minor. So
